Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T77 (ledgerRefs goals:G5)
Branch: implement/T77    Base commit: c30ac49 (current main HEAD). If the harness cut your worktree from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard c30ac49` first (sanctioned base-refresh); confirm internal/dnsresolve/ and internal/device/resolution.go exist before starting.
headline: Add the netns e2e: dial-by-name, mid-session concentrator IP change, tunnel survives
description: The v1 acceptance bar (Q36). Extend the privileged netns e2e suite (build on test/e2e/failover_test.go's mid-session-switch harness): the edge config names the concentrator by HOSTNAME (dns opt-in on, system resolver). Name resolution inside the netns needs a netns-local answer source — /etc/hosts is not netns-scoped for the Go resolver, so run a minimal in-test UDP DNS responder inside the edge namespace (dnsmessage-based, reusing the package helpers) and point the edge at it (resolv.conf in a mount namespace, or the [dns] dot/doh server override if simpler — choose and document). Scenario: (0) edge BOOTS while the name is UNRESOLVABLE (responder down/NXDOMAIN): tunnel comes up endpoint-less (tolerant boot, no crash); (1) responder starts answering, edge's FIRST successful resolve installs the engine peer endpoint and the first handshake goes through — tunnel up, traffic flows (R70 end-to-end); (2) concentrator's IP CHANGES mid-session (move the address in the conc namespace + update the DNS answer); (3) edge re-resolves (poll or liveness-loss trigger) and repoints; (4) tunnel SURVIVES — post-change traffic flows within a bounded window; also assert the D32 regression guard (resequencer re-baselines — traffic actually resumes, not just a handshake).
acceptance: go test -tags e2e -run DNS (or the chosen name) passes as root on the e2e hosts; edge boots name-unresolvable and reaches tunnel-up only after the responder answers (R70), then IP change mid-session, post-change traffic resumes within the poll+settle bound; hermetic to the netns sandbox (no external DNS egress).

IMPORTANT: the privileged -tags e2e netns suite needs root/CAP_NET_ADMIN hardware you CANNOT run here — write the e2e test + in-test DNS responder so they COMPILE and vet under -tags e2e (`go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e` + `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e` must pass). Note e2e execution is DEFERRED to the o3.7mind.io + llm-ubuntu-0 hosts (G2 pattern). Read test/e2e/failover_test.go (the mid-session-switch harness), test/e2e/netns.go (Topology helpers), and internal/dnsresolve (System/DoH/DoT, dnscodec) for the DNS-responder idiom.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes.
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t77-fix` off c30ac49 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.

- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	//go:build e2e
2	
3	package e2e
4	
5	import (
6		"encoding/json"
7		"fmt"
8		"os"
9		"os/exec"
10		"strconv"
11		"strings"
12		"testing"
13		"time"
14	)
15	
16	// TestP1Failover is the P1 failover-recovery acceptance and the D15 regression
17	// guard: with a SATURATING bidirectional bulk flow loading both ends, the ACTIVE
18	// WAN is killed and the bond must reroute egress in BOTH directions within
19	// P1RecoverySeconds — reliably, with margin, not just on a lucky run — and the flow
20	// must survive (no WireGuard-session reset).
21	//
22	// What is measured, and why THIS way. Recovery is "throughput restored in both
23	// directions after the active WAN dies". Bidirectional traffic is restored the
24	// instant BOTH ends have rerouted egress off the dead path; the reroute itself is a
25	// sub-millisecond Pick()/data-structure update, so the recovery latency in each
26	// direction is precisely when that end's scheduler logs its "active path change"
27	// failover. The test reads those two timestamps from the two daemons' logs — a
28	// sub-millisecond, un-confounded measurement — and takes recovery = max(edge, conc):
29	//
30	//   - edge_switch is the FORWARD direction (edge egress → backup);
31	//   - conc_switch is the REPLY direction (concentrator egress → backup) — the term
32	//     D15/D16 previously under-budgeted and the one that tailed past 3s under load.
33	//
34	// A DATA-plane ping-gap probe was rejected as the timing metric: on the emulated
35	// single post-failover path it shares one netem queue with the saturating flow and
36	// is tail-dropped for seconds, which measures congestion, not failover. Instead the
37	// saturating flow IS the data-plane proof: it spans the kill and must complete with
38	// positive throughput in both directions, proving the one WireGuard session (hence
39	// the flow) survived the reroute. (Cross-check: with the load removed a clean
40	// 20ms-cadence ping gap recovers in ~1.5s, matching the switch latencies — the log
41	// metric is a faithful proxy for data-plane recovery, just without the confound.)
42	//
43	// The load is what made the D15 tail jitter: the concentrator absorbing a saturating
44	// flood on 4 vCPU starved its probe-loop ticker, delaying the reply-direction detect.
45	// The T39 fix advances liveness off the receive path too, so the concentrator-side
46	// switch no longer waits on that starved timer.
47	//
48	// Run it MANY times to characterise the tail: `-run TestP1Failover -count=20`. Each
49	// -count iteration is an independent bring-up + kill + measure; the per-run
50	// `RECOVERY_MS=` line is parseable for the pass-rate/distribution report.
51	func TestP1Failover(t *testing.T) {
52		bin := buildWanbond(t)
53		top := Setup(t)
54		edge, conc := setupMultipathTunnelLevel(t, top, bin, DefaultPaths, "info")
55	
56		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
57			t.Fatalf("bond never came up\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", edge.log(), conc.log())
58		}
59	
60		primary := DefaultPaths[0]   // starlink — the active-backup primary (all egress rides it)
61		secondary := DefaultPaths[1] // cellular — the failover backup
62	
63		// Let both ends settle so the primary is the established active path on BOTH the
64		// edge (egress) and the concentrator (replies) before the kill.
65		time.Sleep(1500 * time.Millisecond)
66	
67		// A saturating bidirectional bulk flow spans the whole window: it recreates the
68		// D15 CPU load AND is the data-plane-survival proof (it must finish with positive
69		// throughput both ways). It is uncapped, so both ends' WG crypto runs flat out.
70		const loadSecs = 12
71		top.startProc(t, "iperf3-server", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", "iperf3", "-s", "-1", "-B", concInner)
72		time.Sleep(400 * time.Millisecond)
73		load := exec.Command("iperf3", "-c", concInner, "-t", strconv.Itoa(loadSecs), "--bidir", "-J")
74		loadOut := &lockedBuffer{}
75		load.Stdout, load.Stderr = loadOut, loadOut
76		if err := load.Start(); err != nil {
77			t.Fatalf("start load flow: %v", err)
78		}
79		// Reap the saturating flow on EVERY exit path, not just load.Wait() on success: a
80		// Fatalf before Wait would otherwise leak an uncapped ~70s --bidir iperf3 that keeps
81		// saturating the (shared) e2e host and contaminates subsequent/concurrent runs (D21).
82		// Kill after a completed Wait is a harmless no-op.
83		t.Cleanup(func() {
84			if load.Process != nil {
85				_ = load.Process.Kill()
86			}
87		})
88	
89		// Let the flow ramp and both ends reach steady state on the primary, then kill the
90		// active WAN and stamp the instant.
91		time.Sleep(3 * time.Second)
92		killAt := time.Now()
93		top.Blackhole(primary.name)
94		t.Logf("killed active WAN %q at T0", primary.name)
95	
96		// Await the bulk flow: a preserved single WG session keeps the connection across
97		// the reroute, so a healthy failover exits 0 with positive throughput; a session
98		// reset surfaces as a non-zero exit.
99		loadErr := load.Wait()
100		top.Restore(primary.name)
101	
102		// Per-direction failover latency, read from each daemon's scheduler transition.
103		edgeSwitch := schedSwitchLatency(edge.log(), killAt)
104		concSwitch := schedSwitchLatency(conc.log(), killAt)
105		if edgeSwitch < 0 || concSwitch < 0 {
106			t.Fatalf("could not measure both failover switches (edge=%s conc=%s) — no scheduler transition logged after the kill\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
107				latencyStr(edgeSwitch), latencyStr(concSwitch), edge.log(), conc.log())
108		}
109		// End-to-end bidirectional recovery is governed by the SLOWER of the two ends: both
110		// directions carry traffic once both have rerouted.
111		recovery := edgeSwitch
112		if concSwitch > recovery {
113			recovery = concSwitch
114		}
115	
116		// The single parseable metric line the multi-run harness greps.
117		t.Logf("RECOVERY_MS=%d budget_ms=%d failover_budget_ms=%d edge_switch_ms=%d conc_switch_ms=%d",
118			recovery.Milliseconds(), int64(P1RecoverySeconds)*1000, PLivenessFailoverBudget.Milliseconds(),
119			edgeSwitch.Milliseconds(), concSwitch.Milliseconds())
120	
121		// Data-plane survival: the flow that spanned the kill must have carried traffic
122		// both ways with no reset.
123		fwd, rev := iperfBidirMbps(loadOut.String())
124		if loadErr != nil || fwd <= 0 || rev <= 0 {
125			t.Errorf("bulk flow did not survive failover (exit err=%v, forward=%.1f Mbit/s, reverse=%.1f Mbit/s) — a WG-session reset?\n%s",
126				loadErr, fwd, rev, loadOut.String())
127		}
128	
129		// Sanity: the backup carries the recovered bond.
130		if !top.Reachable(secondary.name, 3) {
131			t.Errorf("backup path %q unreachable after failover", secondary.name)
132		}
133	
134		if recovery >= time.Duration(P1RecoverySeconds)*time.Second {
135			t.Errorf("bidirectional recovery %v exceeded P1 budget %ds (edge_switch=%v conc_switch=%v)\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
136				recovery, P1RecoverySeconds, edgeSwitch, concSwitch, edge.log(), conc.log())
137		}
138	}
139	
140	// P1 active-backup priority indices: the scheduler's health slice is priority-
141	// ordered, index 0 the preferred primary and index 1 the failover backup — matching
142	// the config path order (DefaultPaths[0] primary, DefaultPaths[1] backup). The
143	// "scheduler active path change" log records the destination index as its "to"
144	// field, so a to==primaryPathIdx transition is a failback and to==backupPathIdx is a
145	// failover.
146	const (
147		primaryPathIdx = 0
148		backupPathIdx  = 1
149	)
150	
151	// TestP1FailoverRepeatedFlap is the SECOND half of the T20 acceptance that
152	// TestP1Failover (a single kill+restore) does not cover: "repeated flap does not
153	// wedge the tunnel". It runs ONE long-lived saturating bidirectional bulk flow across
154	// SEVERAL kill/restore cycles of the ACTIVE WAN and asserts the tunnel recovers every
155	// cycle — within P1RecoverySeconds, both directions, measured the same sound
156	// per-direction way as TestP1Failover — and that the single flow survives ALL cycles
157	// with no WireGuard-session reset.
158	//
159	// Non-vacuity guard (the opus T20-r1 finding). A repeated-flap test is only meaningful
160	// if each cycle genuinely kills the ACTIVE egress path. After a restore the recovered
161	// primary does not reclaim egress instantly: the scheduler debounces failback with a
162	// 5s dwell (sched.Config.FailbackAfter), so a blind time.Sleep between cycles could
163	// kill an already-idle backup and pass trivially. Instead, before every kill this test
164	// confirms — in BOTH daemons' logs — that egress sits on the primary. For cycles >= 2 it
165	// waits for a FRESH to-primary "scheduler active path change" (to==0) logged after that
166	// cycle's restore: that IS the genuine FAILBACK and the anti-wedge proof for the prior
167	// cycle. Cycle 1 has no prior restore and the lossless primary logs its to-primary
168	// selection only once at cold-start (before any post-setup instant, and never repeated),
169	// so it instead reads the CURRENT active path — the most-recent transition's destination
170	// — and asserts it is the primary. Either way the precondition guarantees the next kill
171	// hits the active path.
172	//
173	// Both daemons run at INFO so those transitions are observable (as in TestP1Failover).
174	// The per-cycle metric line `FLAP_CYCLE=<n> RECOVERY_MS=<ms>` is grep-able for a
175	// pass-rate/distribution report over `-run TestP1FailoverRepeatedFlap -count=N`.
176	func TestP1FailoverRepeatedFlap(t *testing.T) {
177		const (
178			flapCycles = 3
179			// flapFailoverPoll bounds how long we wait to OBSERVE both ends' failover
180			// switch after a kill. It is set WELL ABOVE P1RecoverySeconds (not budget+1s)
181			// so a heavily-late failover is still OBSERVED and MEASURED — then asserted
182			// against the budget with its true magnitude via the per-cycle Errorf below —
183			// rather than lost to an unmeasured non-observation Fatalf. The old budget+1s
184			// (4s) window was the T20-review measurement gap: a genuine >4s recovery tail
185			// fell OUTSIDE it and was reported as "never switched" (an unmeasured Fatalf)
186			// instead of "switched late by N ms" (a measured, magnitude-bearing failure).
187			flapFailoverPoll = time.Duration(P1RecoverySeconds)*time.Second + 5*time.Second
188			// flapFailbackPoll bounds the wait for both ends to fail egress BACK to the
189			// primary after a restore: the 5s FailbackAfter dwell + up-detect (3×200ms) +
190			// margin for the D15 under-load detection tail. If failback does not complete
191			// within this, the tunnel has wedged on the backup.
192			flapFailbackPoll = 12 * time.Second
193			flapRampBefore   = 2500 * time.Millisecond
194		)
195	
196		bin := buildWanbond(t)
197		top := Setup(t)
198		edge, conc := setupMultipathTunnelLevel(t, top, bin, DefaultPaths, "info")
199	
200		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
201			t.Fatalf("bond never came up\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s", edge.log(), conc.log())
202		}
203	
204		primary := DefaultPaths[primaryPathIdx]  // starlink — the active-backup primary
205		secondary := DefaultPaths[backupPathIdx] // cellular — the failover backup
206	
207		// One saturating bidirectional flow spans EVERY cycle: it recreates the D15 CPU
208		// load and is the data-plane-survival proof — it must finish (exit 0) with positive
209		// throughput both ways, proving the one WireGuard session (hence the flow) survived
210		// all the reroutes. Size its lifetime to the worst-case cycle budget so it is still
211		// running throughout the loop no matter how the per-cycle waits resolve.
212		loadWindow := flapRampBefore +
213			time.Duration(flapCycles)*(flapFailoverPoll+flapFailbackPoll+time.Second) +
214			4*time.Second
215		loadSecs := int(loadWindow.Seconds()) + 1
216	
217		top.startProc(t, "iperf3-server", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", "iperf3", "-s", "-1", "-B", concInner)
218		time.Sleep(400 * time.Millisecond)
219		load := exec.Command("iperf3", "-c", concInner, "-t", strconv.Itoa(loadSecs), "--bidir", "-J")
220		loadOut := &lockedBuffer{}
221		load.Stdout, load.Stderr = loadOut, loadOut
222		if err := load.Start(); err != nil {
223			t.Fatalf("start load flow: %v", err)
224		}
225		// Reap the saturating flow on EVERY exit path, not just the load.Wait() on success:
226		// an early Fatalf (a failover non-observation or a failback wedge) before Wait would
227		// otherwise leak an uncapped ~70s --bidir iperf3 that keeps saturating the shared e2e
228		// host and contaminates subsequent/concurrent runs (D21). Kill after a completed Wait
229		// is a harmless no-op.
230		t.Cleanup(func() {
231			if load.Process != nil {
232				_ = load.Process.Kill()
233			}
234		})
235	
236		// Let the flow ramp and both ends reach steady state on the primary before cycle 1.
237		time.Sleep(flapRampBefore)
238	
239		// sinceRef is the reference instant after which a to-primary transition confirms
240		// egress has (re)claimed the primary before the next kill. It is used only for
241		// cycles >= 2, where it is the PREVIOUS cycle's restore instant, so each confirmation
242		// is that cycle's genuine FAILBACK (a to-primary transition logged strictly after the
243		// restore). Cycle 1 does not use it — see the cycle-1 branch below — so its zero
244		// value is never read.
245		var sinceRef time.Time
246	
247		for cycle := 1; cycle <= flapCycles; cycle++ {
248			// Precondition (non-vacuity): egress must be on the primary on BOTH ends before
249			// we kill it, so the kill genuinely hits the ACTIVE path. This is also the
250			// anti-wedge assertion for the prior cycle.
251			if cycle == 1 {
252				// Cycle 1 cannot demand a FRESH to-primary transition: the cold-start
253				// selection to the primary (from:-1→to:0) is logged during bring-up, before
254				// any instant we could stamp after setup, and — DefaultPaths being lossless —
255				// the primary never re-flaps, so no further to-primary transition is ever
256				// logged. A windowed waitBothSwitchTo would therefore time out with a FALSE
257				// wedge before any kill. Instead assert the CURRENT active path is the primary
258				// by reading the most-recent transition's destination on both ends — robust to
259				// the cold-start transition predating any reference instant, and the true
260				// non-vacuity precondition (both ends are on the primary now).
261				if !waitBothActiveOn(edge, conc, primaryPathIdx, flapFailbackPoll) {
262					t.Fatalf("cycle 1: egress not on the primary on both ends within %v at start — cold-start selection never settled on the primary\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
263						flapFailbackPoll, edge.log(), conc.log())
264				}
265			} else if _, _, ok := waitBothSwitchTo(edge, conc, sinceRef, primaryPathIdx, flapFailbackPoll); !ok {
266				t.Fatalf("cycle %d: egress never (re)claimed the primary on both ends within %v — tunnel wedged on the backup after the prior cycle\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
267					cycle, flapFailbackPoll, edge.log(), conc.log())
268			}
269	
270			// Kill the ACTIVE primary and stamp T0 for this cycle.
271			killAt := time.Now()
272			top.Blackhole(primary.name)
273	
274			// Per-direction failover latency, read from each daemon's to-backup transition
275			// after this cycle's kill — the same sound, un-confounded measurement as
276			// TestP1Failover. End-to-end bidirectional recovery is the SLOWER of the two.
277			edgeSwitch, concSwitch, ok := waitBothSwitchTo(edge, conc, killAt, backupPathIdx, flapFailoverPoll)
278			if !ok {
279				top.Restore(primary.name)
280				t.Fatalf("cycle %d: both ends did not fail over to the backup within %v (edge=%s conc=%s %s) — no scheduler transition logged after the kill\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
281					cycle, flapFailoverPoll, latencyStr(edgeSwitch), latencyStr(concSwitch), readLoadAvg(), edge.log(), conc.log())
282			}
283			recovery := edgeSwitch
284			if concSwitch > recovery {
285				recovery = concSwitch
286			}
287			// Record host load on the metric line: the repeated-flap tail is sensitive to
288			// shared-VM CPU contention (4 vCPU, possibly multi-tenant), so every per-cycle
289			// measurement carries the load that produced it — that is what lets a genuine
290			// product tail be told apart from host-contention noise in the run log (D18).
291			t.Logf("FLAP_CYCLE=%d RECOVERY_MS=%d budget_ms=%d edge_switch_ms=%d conc_switch_ms=%d %s",
292				cycle, recovery.Milliseconds(), int64(P1RecoverySeconds)*1000,
293				edgeSwitch.Milliseconds(), concSwitch.Milliseconds(), readLoadAvg())
294			if recovery >= time.Duration(P1RecoverySeconds)*time.Second {
295				t.Errorf("cycle %d: bidirectional recovery %v exceeded P1 budget %ds (edge_switch=%v conc_switch=%v %s)",
296					cycle, recovery, P1RecoverySeconds, edgeSwitch, concSwitch, readLoadAvg())
297			}
298	
299			// Restore the primary; the next iteration's precondition wait confirms failback.
300			restoreAt := time.Now()
301			top.Restore(primary.name)
302			sinceRef = restoreAt
303		}
304	
305		// Final anti-wedge check: after the last restore the bond must return to the
306		// primary too (the loop's top-of-cycle check does not cover the last cycle).
307		if _, _, ok := waitBothSwitchTo(edge, conc, sinceRef, primaryPathIdx, flapFailbackPoll); !ok {
308			t.Errorf("after the final cycle egress never returned to the primary on both ends within %v — tunnel wedged on the backup\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
309				flapFailbackPoll, edge.log(), conc.log())
310		}
311	
312		// Data-plane survival across ALL cycles: the one flow that spanned every kill must
313		// have completed with traffic both ways and no reset.
314		loadErr := load.Wait()
315		fwd, rev := iperfBidirMbps(loadOut.String())
316		if loadErr != nil || fwd <= 0 || rev <= 0 {
317			t.Errorf("bulk flow did not survive %d failover cycles (exit err=%v, forward=%.1f Mbit/s, reverse=%.1f Mbit/s) — a WG-session reset?\n%s",
318				flapCycles, loadErr, fwd, rev, loadOut.String())
319		}
320	
321		// Sanity: the backup path is still reachable (it carried the bond during each cycle).
322		if !top.Reachable(secondary.name, 3) {
323			t.Errorf("backup path %q unreachable after repeated flap", secondary.name)
324		}
325	
326		t.Logf("repeated-flap: survived %d kill/restore cycles of the active WAN with one spanning flow (forward=%.1f Mbit/s reverse=%.1f Mbit/s), egress failed back to the primary each cycle",
327			flapCycles, fwd, rev)
328	}
329	
330	// waitBothSwitchTo polls both daemons' logs until EACH has logged a "scheduler active
331	// path change" whose destination is toIdx at some instant strictly after `after`, or
332	// the deadline elapses. It returns the two per-daemon latencies from `after` to that
333	// transition (or -1 for a daemon that never switched) and whether both were observed.
334	// It is used both to MEASURE failover (toIdx = backupPathIdx, from the kill instant)
335	// and to CONFIRM failback (toIdx = primaryPathIdx, from the restore instant) before the
336	// next kill.
337	func waitBothSwitchTo(edge, conc *proc, after time.Time, toIdx int, deadline time.Duration) (edgeLat, concLat time.Duration, ok bool) {
338		stop := time.Now().Add(deadline)
339		for {
340			edgeLat = switchToLatency(edge.log(), after, toIdx)
341			concLat = switchToLatency(conc.log(), after, toIdx)
342			if edgeLat >= 0 && concLat >= 0 {
343				return edgeLat, concLat, true
344			}
345			if time.Now().After(stop) {
346				return edgeLat, concLat, false
347			}
348			time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
349		}
350	}
351	
352	// currentActivePathIdx returns the destination index of the MOST-RECENT "scheduler
353	// active path change" transition in a daemon's log, or -1 if none has been logged. It
354	// is NOT windowed by an instant: it reports the scheduler's CURRENT active path
355	// regardless of when that transition was logged. Cycle 1's precondition uses it to
356	// confirm the cold-start selection put egress on the primary WITHOUT demanding a fresh
357	// post-setup transition — the lossless primary logs its to-primary selection only once
358	// at bring-up and never re-flaps, so no such fresh transition ever exists.
359	func currentActivePathIdx(logText string) int {
360		idx := -1
361		var latest time.Time
362		for _, line := range strings.Split(logText, "\n") {
363			if !strings.Contains(line, "scheduler active path change") {
364				continue
365			}
366			var rec struct {
367				Time time.Time `json:"time"`
368				To   int       `json:"to"`
369			}
370			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &rec); err != nil {
371				continue
372			}
373			if idx < 0 || rec.Time.After(latest) {
374				latest = rec.Time
375				idx = rec.To
376			}
377		}
378		return idx
379	}
380	
381	// waitBothActiveOn polls both daemons' logs until EACH reports its CURRENT active path
382	// (the most-recent transition's destination) is toIdx, or the deadline elapses. Unlike
383	// waitBothSwitchTo it asserts the PRESENT active path rather than a fresh transition
384	// after some instant, so it confirms cycle 1's cold-start selection without requiring a
385	// to-primary transition logged after a reference time.
386	func waitBothActiveOn(edge, conc *proc, toIdx int, deadline time.Duration) bool {
387		stop := time.Now().Add(deadline)
388		for {
389			if currentActivePathIdx(edge.log()) == toIdx && currentActivePathIdx(conc.log()) == toIdx {
390				return true
391			}
392			if time.Now().After(stop) {
393				return false
394			}
395			time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
396		}
397	}
398	
399	// switchToLatency returns the delay from `after` to the EARLIEST "scheduler active
400	// path change" transition to destination index toIdx logged strictly after `after`, or
401	// -1 if none. It is schedSwitchLatency refined by the transition's "to" field, so a
402	// failover (to the backup) and a failback (to the primary) can be told apart in a log
403	// that accumulates both across repeated cycles.
404	func switchToLatency(logText string, after time.Time, toIdx int) time.Duration {
405		best := time.Duration(-1)
406		for _, line := range strings.Split(logText, "\n") {
407			if !strings.Contains(line, "scheduler active path change") {
408				continue
409			}
410			var rec struct {
411				Time time.Time `json:"time"`
412				To   int       `json:"to"`
413			}
414			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &rec); err != nil {
415				continue
416			}
417			if rec.To != toIdx || !rec.Time.After(after) {
418				continue
419			}
420			d := rec.Time.Sub(after)
421			if best < 0 || d < best {
422				best = d
423			}
424		}
425		return best
426	}
427	
428	// schedSwitchLatency returns the delay from killAt to the first "scheduler active
429	// path change" transition in a daemon's JSON log after the kill, or -1 if none is
430	// found (the daemon log is slog JSON: {"time":...,"msg":"scheduler active path
431	// change",...}). It is the per-direction failover-recovery latency: the reroute is a
432	// sub-ms update, so the transition timestamp is when that end's egress moves to the
433	// surviving path.
434	func schedSwitchLatency(logText string, killAt time.Time) time.Duration {
435		best := time.Duration(-1)
436		for _, line := range strings.Split(logText, "\n") {
437			if !strings.Contains(line, "scheduler active path change") {
438				continue
439			}
440			var rec struct {
441				Time time.Time `json:"time"`
442			}
443			if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(line), &rec); err != nil {
444				continue
445			}
446			if rec.Time.Before(killAt) {
447				continue // the initial cold-start selection, not the failover
448			}
449			d := rec.Time.Sub(killAt)
450			if best < 0 || d < best {
451				best = d
452			}
453		}
454		return best
455	}
456	
457	// iperfBidirMbps parses an iperf3 --bidir -J report, returning the forward
458	// (client→server, sum_sent) and reverse (server→client, sum_received) throughput in
459	// Mbit/s. Zero for either direction (or an unparseable report) signals the flow did
460	// not carry traffic that way.
461	func iperfBidirMbps(out string) (forward, reverse float64) {
462		var r struct {
463			End struct {
464				SumSent struct {
465					BitsPerSecond float64 `json:"bits_per_second"`
466				} `json:"sum_sent"`
467				SumReceived struct {
468					BitsPerSecond float64 `json:"bits_per_second"`
469				} `json:"sum_received"`
470			} `json:"end"`
471		}
472		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(out), &r); err != nil {
473			return 0, 0
474		}
475		return r.End.SumSent.BitsPerSecond / 1e6, r.End.SumReceived.BitsPerSecond / 1e6
476	}
477	
478	// latencyStr renders a measured latency, or "n/a" for a missing (-1) measurement.
479	func latencyStr(d time.Duration) string {
480		if d < 0 {
481			return "n/a"
482		}
483		return fmt.Sprintf("%d", d.Milliseconds())
484	}
485	
486	// readLoadAvg returns the host's 1/5/15-minute load averages as a compact
487	// "load=1min,5min,15min" token (or "load=?" if /proc/loadavg is unreadable). The
488	// repeated-flap failover tail is sensitive to shared-VM CPU contention — the e2e host
489	// runs on 4 vCPU and may be multi-tenant — so every per-cycle metric line and every
490	// budget-exceeded/non-observation failure stamps the load that accompanied it. That
491	// is the robustness the D18 investigation added: a future over-budget cycle is
492	// self-classifying from the log alone (a high load average points at host contention;
493	// a low one at a genuine product regression) instead of needing an out-of-band host
494	// snapshot that no longer exists by the time the failure is read.
495	func readLoadAvg() string {
496		b, err := os.ReadFile("/proc/loadavg")
497		if err != nil {
498			return "load=?"
499		}
500		f := strings.Fields(string(b))
501		if len(f) < 3 {
502			return "load=?"
503		}
504		return fmt.Sprintf("load=%s,%s,%s", f[0], f[1], f[2])
505	}

1	//go:build e2e

3	package e2e
4	
5	import (
6		"fmt"
7		"os"
8		"os/exec"
9		"strconv"
10		"strings"
11		"testing"
12		"time"
13	)
14	
15	// pathSpec describes one emulated WAN uplink between the edge and concentrator
16	// namespaces: a veth pair carrying a /24, with netem delay+jitter on the edge
17	// egress. The bandwidth cap (rateMbit) and controlled loss (lossPct) are
18	// OPTIONAL config-time impairments: both default to zero, so the DefaultPaths
19	// topology stays uncapped and lossless and every existing P0/P1 e2e test runs
20	// unchanged. A non-zero rateMbit makes the LINK — not the single-core userspace
21	// WG crypto — the bottleneck ONLY when rateMbit sits below the EXECUTING host's
22	// measured in-fixture tunnel ceiling. That ceiling is CPU/PPS-bound (both daemons
23	// plus the load generator share the host's cores), a lower bound that scales with
24	// core count, NOT a link-throughput spec: ~12–46 Mbit/s single-flow on a 1-vCPU
25	// aarch64 host (docs/p0-findings.md:216-225), ~13 Mbit/s single-path (up to
26	// ~47–87 Mbit/s FEC single-flow) on a 4-vCPU amd64 host. Sizing rule: cap < ceiling
27	// for single-path, 2×cap < ceiling for aggregation. Below the ceiling
28	// a standing queue can form for bufferbloat/pacing (T21/T23) work; a non-zero
29	// lossPct injects uniform egress loss at Setup time for FEC-recovery (T25/T29)
30	// work.
31	type pathSpec struct {
32		name     string
33		edgeIP   string
34		concIP   string
35		edgeVeth string // <=15 chars
36		concVeth string
37		delayMs  int
38		jitterMs int
39		rateMbit int     // optional per-path bandwidth cap (netem rate); 0 = uncapped
40		lossPct  float64 // optional config-time uniform egress loss (netem loss); 0 = lossless
41	
42		// deferEdgeAddr, when true, makes Setup create and bring up the edge veth WITHOUT
43		// assigning edgeIP to it: the interface exists (so tc/netem still applies and the
44		// link is up) but the configured source_addr is not yet owned by any interface —
45		// the well-formed-but-not-yet-assignable condition T51's tolerant Open() defers and
46		// T55's background reconcile later promotes (T60). AddEdgeAddr adds the withheld
47		// address later. Zero value (false) is the default for every existing path, so
48		// Setup's behaviour for DefaultPaths and every other caller is unchanged.
49		deferEdgeAddr bool
50	}
51	
52	// DefaultPaths are the two emulated links: Starlink-like (low latency, jittery)
53	// and 5G-like (higher latency, stable).
54	var DefaultPaths = []pathSpec{
55		{name: "starlink", edgeIP: "10.100.1.1", concIP: "10.100.1.2", edgeVeth: "wbAe", concVeth: "wbAc", delayMs: 45, jitterMs: 10},
56		{name: "cellular", edgeIP: "10.100.2.1", concIP: "10.100.2.2", edgeVeth: "wbBe", concVeth: "wbBc", delayMs: 64, jitterMs: 0},
57	}
58	
59	// Topology is a two-namespace netns/netem fixture. The edge side is the current
60	// (re-exec'd) network namespace; the concentrator side is a child process's
61	// network namespace, addressed by PID so no writable /run/netns is required.
62	type Topology struct {
63		t      *testing.T
64		holder *exec.Cmd
65		pid    int
66		paths  []pathSpec
67	}
68	
69	// Setup builds the two-path topology from DefaultPaths (uncapped, lossless). It
70	// requires CAP_NET_ADMIN, which the e2e TestMain provides via an unprivileged
71	// user+net namespace (`unshare -Urmn`).
72	func Setup(t *testing.T) *Topology {
73		return SetupWithPaths(t, DefaultPaths)
74	}
75	
76	// SetupWithPaths builds the topology from an explicit path set, allowing a test
77	// to opt into the optional per-path bandwidth cap (rateMbit) and/or config-time
78	// loss (lossPct). DefaultPaths leaves both zero, so Setup is unchanged.
79	func SetupWithPaths(t *testing.T, paths []pathSpec) *Topology {
80		t.Helper()
81		top := &Topology{t: t, paths: paths}
82	
83		// Hold the concentrator network namespace open with a sleeping child.
84		top.holder = exec.Command("unshare", "-n", "sleep", "600")
85		if err := top.holder.Start(); err != nil {
86			t.Fatalf("start concentrator netns holder: %v", err)
87		}
88		top.pid = top.holder.Process.Pid
89		top.waitForNetns()
90	
91		pid := strconv.Itoa(top.pid)
92		top.run("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
93		top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up")
94		// Pin net.ipv4.ip_nonlocal_bind=0 in the edge (test-process) network namespace, so
95		// a path whose source_addr is NOT assigned to any interface deterministically fails
96		// to bind (EADDRNOTAVAIL) instead of succeeding. That non-local-bind FAILURE is the
97		// premise the tolerant-startup tests (T60) rest on: a not-yet-assignable source_addr
98		// must DEFER (T51) and a config where NO path can bind must fail Open. A host that
99		// leaves the sysctl at 1 (or runs permissively) lets a non-local bind SUCCEED, which
100		// silently voids both premises (observed nondeterministic on a real host: a deferred
101		// path binding instead of deferring, or a zero-bindable daemon coming up). A fresh
102		// netns SHOULD default to 0, but pin it explicitly rather than trust the default.
103		// This affects ONLY non-local binds, so every other e2e test — which binds real,
104		// assigned addresses — is byte-for-byte unaffected. The concentrator netns needs no
105		// pin: every concentrator source_addr (concIP) IS assigned there (deferEdgeAddr only
106		// withholds the EDGE address), so the concentrator never binds a non-local address.
107		disableNonlocalBind(top.t)
108		for _, p := range top.paths {
109			// Idempotent pre-delete: the veth names are FIXED per path, so a prior subtest's
110			// teardown racing the kernel's async netns/veth reap can leave the edge veth behind,
111			// making `ip link add` fail with EEXIST ("File exists"). Deleting the pair first
112			// (ignore-if-absent) makes Setup robust to reused fixed names across sequential
113			// subtests. Deleting the edge end removes its peer too.
114			_ = top.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", p.edgeVeth)
115			top.run("ip", "link", "add", p.edgeVeth, "type", "veth", "peer", "name", p.concVeth)
116			top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.concVeth, "netns", pid)
117			if !p.deferEdgeAddr {
118				top.run("ip", "addr", "add", p.edgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
119			}
120			top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
121			top.nsenter("ip", "addr", "add", p.concIP+"/24", "dev", p.concVeth)
122			top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", p.concVeth, "up")
123			qargs := append([]string{"qdisc", "add", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgs(p)...)
124			top.run("tc", qargs...)
125		}
126		t.Cleanup(top.Teardown)
127		return top
128	}
129	
130	// waitForNetns blocks until the holder's network namespace is observable.
131	func (top *Topology) waitForNetns() {
132		path := fmt.Sprintf("/proc/%d/ns/net", top.pid)
133		for i := 0; i < 100; i++ {
134			if _, err := os.Stat(path); err == nil {
135				return
136			}
137			time.Sleep(10 * time.Millisecond)
138		}
139		top.t.Fatalf("concentrator netns %s never appeared", path)
140	}
141	
142	// netemArgs builds the netem parameter list for a path's baseline impairment
143	// profile: delay/jitter, then its configured loss (lossPct) and bandwidth cap
144	// (rateMbit) if set. For a DefaultPaths entry (both zero) this reduces to the
145	// prior delay/jitter-only output, so existing qdisc setup is byte-identical.
146	func (top *Topology) netemArgs(p pathSpec) []string {
147		return top.netemArgsWithLoss(p, p.lossPct)
148	}
149	
150	// netemArgsWithLoss is netemArgs with the loss percentage overridden — used by
151	// InjectLoss to set runtime loss while preserving delay/jitter and any rate cap.
152	// netem accepts the options in delay/loss/rate order.
153	func (top *Topology) netemArgsWithLoss(p pathSpec, lossPct float64) []string {
154		args := []string{"delay", fmt.Sprintf("%dms", p.delayMs)}
155		if p.jitterMs > 0 {
156			args = append(args, fmt.Sprintf("%dms", p.jitterMs))
157		}
158		if lossPct > 0 {
159			args = append(args, "loss", fmt.Sprintf("%g%%", lossPct))
160		}
161		if p.rateMbit > 0 {
162			args = append(args, "rate", fmt.Sprintf("%dmbit", p.rateMbit))
163		}
164		return args
165	}
166	
167	// path looks up a path by name, failing the test if absent.
168	func (top *Topology) path(name string) pathSpec {
169		for _, p := range top.paths {
170			if p.name == name {
171				return p
172			}
173		}
174		top.t.Fatalf("unknown path %q", name)
175		return pathSpec{}
176	}
177	
178	// RTT pings the concentrator end of the named path from the edge and returns the
179	// average round-trip time in milliseconds.
180	func (top *Topology) RTT(name string, count int) float64 {
181		p := top.path(name)
182		out := top.runOut("ping", "-c", strconv.Itoa(count), "-i", "0.2", "-W", "1", p.concIP)
183		// rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 37.264/48.520/82.043/19.354 ms
184		idx := strings.Index(out, "min/avg/max")
185		if idx < 0 {
186			top.t.Fatalf("path %q: no rtt line in ping output:\n%s", name, out)
187		}
188		eq := strings.Index(out[idx:], "=")
189		fields := strings.Fields(out[idx+eq+1:])
190		nums := strings.Split(fields[0], "/")
191		avg, err := strconv.ParseFloat(nums[1], 64)
192		if err != nil {
193			top.t.Fatalf("path %q: parse avg rtt %q: %v", name, nums[1], err)
194		}
195		return avg
196	}
197	
198	// Reachable reports whether the concentrator end of the named path answers pings.
199	func (top *Topology) Reachable(name string, count int) bool {
200		p := top.path(name)
201		return top.tryRun("ping", "-c", strconv.Itoa(count), "-i", "0.2", "-W", "1", p.concIP) == nil
202	}
203	
204	// InjectLoss sets uniform egress loss (percent) on the named path at runtime,
205	// preserving its delay/jitter profile and any configured bandwidth cap.
206	func (top *Topology) InjectLoss(name string, pct float64) {
207		p := top.path(name)
208		args := append([]string{"qdisc", "change", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgsWithLoss(p, pct)...)
209		top.run("tc", args...)
210	}
211	
212	// ClearLoss restores the named path to its configured baseline impairment
213	// (delay/jitter, plus any config-time loss and bandwidth cap). For a DefaultPaths
214	// entry that is delay/jitter only.
215	func (top *Topology) ClearLoss(name string) {
216		p := top.path(name)
217		args := append([]string{"qdisc", "change", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "root", "netem"}, top.netemArgs(p)...)
218		top.run("tc", args...)
219	}
220	
221	// Blackhole brings the named path's edge link down, simulating a WAN death (used
222	// by the P1 failover test).
223	func (top *Topology) Blackhole(name string) {
224		p := top.path(name)
225		top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "down")
226	}
227	
228	// Restore brings the named path's edge link back up.
229	func (top *Topology) Restore(name string) {
230		p := top.path(name)
231		top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
232	}
233	
234	// BlockEgress drops ALL outbound traffic on the named path's edge veth via a tc
235	// clsact egress filter (matchall + drop), while leaving the veth up and inbound
236	// traffic untouched — a one-way egress failure (e.g. a dead uplink amplifier), as
237	// opposed to Blackhole's bidirectional link-down. It is used by T104 to prove
238	// liveness is genuinely bidirectional: with only egress dead the peer's own probes
239	// still ARRIVE at this path (an operator naively trusting "receiving traffic" as
240	// health would stay wrongly up), but this path's OWN probe/echo round trip cannot
241	// complete, so it must go DOWN. clsact attaches its own ingress/egress hooks
242	// independent of whatever root qdisc is set, so it coexists with the path's netem
243	// root qdisc (delay/jitter/loss) without disturbing it.
244	func (top *Topology) BlockEgress(name string) {
245		p := top.path(name)
246		top.run("tc", "qdisc", "add", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "clsact")
247		top.run("tc", "filter", "add", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "egress", "protocol", "all", "matchall", "action", "drop")
248	}
249	
250	// UnblockEgress removes a BlockEgress filter, restoring the named path's egress.
251	// Idempotent — deleting an already-absent clsact qdisc is tolerated — so a test may
252	// call it both explicitly and via t.Cleanup without failing on the second call.
253	func (top *Topology) UnblockEgress(name string) {
254		p := top.path(name)
255		_ = top.tryRun("tc", "qdisc", "del", "dev", p.edgeVeth, "clsact")
256	}
257	
258	// Readdress replaces the edge-side address of the named path, simulating an edge
259	// public-IP change (used by the P1 roaming test). newEdgeIP must be a bare IPv4
260	// address in the path's /24.
261	func (top *Topology) Readdress(name, newEdgeIP string) {
262		p := top.path(name)
263		top.run("ip", "addr", "flush", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
264		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", newEdgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
265		top.run("ip", "link", "set", p.edgeVeth, "up")
266	}
267	
268	// AddEdgeAddr adds the named path's configured edge-side address to its (already up)
269	// edge veth, WITHOUT flushing any existing address first (T60). It is the companion
270	// to a path built with deferEdgeAddr: that path's interface starts addressless, so
271	// this simulates the address becoming assignable after boot (e.g. a 5G modem's DHCP
272	// lease completing) for the T55 background reconciler to observe. Unlike Readdress —
273	// which flushes THEN re-adds, for the T16 re-roam case where an address is replaced —
274	// this only ADDS, because here there is nothing to flush.
275	func (top *Topology) AddEdgeAddr(name string) {
276		p := top.path(name)
277		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", p.edgeIP+"/24", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
278	}
279	
280	// nonlocalBindProc is the per-netns sysctl controlling whether a socket may bind a
281	// source address that is NOT assigned to any local interface. 0 (the value the
282	// tolerant-startup tests pin) makes such a bind fail EADDRNOTAVAIL; 1 lets it
283	// succeed. It is a plain proc file, so it is written/read directly rather than via
284	// the `sysctl` binary (one fewer external dependency).
285	const nonlocalBindProc = "/proc/sys/net/ipv4/ip_nonlocal_bind"
286	
287	// disableNonlocalBind pins ip_nonlocal_bind=0 in the CURRENT (edge/test) network
288	// namespace and asserts the value took, failing the test loudly if it cannot be set.
289	// A hard failure here — rather than proceeding — is deliberate: the tolerant-startup
290	// premise (a non-local bind must fail) is silently voided if the sysctl stays at 1,
291	// so an un-pinnable environment must surface as a test failure, never a false pass.
292	// os.WriteFile targets THIS process's netns, which is the edge/test namespace (the
293	// TestMain re-exec unshared it), exactly where the not-yet-assignable source_addr
294	// binds live. Writing a per-netns net sysctl needs the same privilege the fixture's
295	// veth/netem setup already requires, so anywhere the netns tier runs, this succeeds.
296	func disableNonlocalBind(t *testing.T) {
297		t.Helper()
298		if err := os.WriteFile(nonlocalBindProc, []byte("0\n"), 0o644); err != nil {
299			t.Fatalf("pin %s=0: %v (the tolerant-startup tests need a non-local bind to fail EADDRNOTAVAIL; without this a permissive host silently voids the premise)", nonlocalBindProc, err)
300		}
301		got, err := os.ReadFile(nonlocalBindProc)
302		if err != nil {
303			t.Fatalf("read back %s: %v", nonlocalBindProc, err)
304		}
305		if v := strings.TrimSpace(string(got)); v != "0" {
306			t.Fatalf("%s = %q after pin, want 0 — a non-local bind would still succeed and void the zero-bindable/deferred premise", nonlocalBindProc, v)
307		}
308	}
309	
310	// bringLoopbackUp brings the loopback interface UP in the CURRENT (edge/test)
311	// network namespace. It is a second, INDEPENDENT precondition for a non-local bind
312	// to fail EADDRNOTAVAIL: on this kernel the rejection fires only when at least one
313	// interface is UP — empirically, with lo DOWN a bind to a non-local address SUCCEEDS
314	// even at ip_nonlocal_bind=0 (kernel probe: lo-up+pin0 -> EADDRNOTAVAIL; lo-down+pin0
315	// -> BIND_OK; lo-up+pin1 -> BIND_OK). So the zero-bindable premise needs BOTH lo UP
316	// and the sysctl pinned to 0. SetupWithPaths already brings lo up for topology tests
317	// (line above), which is why the zero-bindable subtest passed only when it happened to
318	// run AFTER one; building no topology itself, it must bring lo up explicitly to hold
319	// regardless of subtest execution order. Idempotent (setting an already-up lo up is a
320	// no-op). Runs in this process's netns (the TestMain re-exec unshared it), like run().
321	func bringLoopbackUp(t *testing.T) {
322		t.Helper()
323		if out, err := exec.Command("ip", "link", "set", "lo", "up").CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
324			t.Fatalf("bring lo up in the test netns: %v\n%s", err, out)
325		}
326	}
327	
328	// QdiscShow returns `tc qdisc show` for the named path (for assertions/debug).
329	func (top *Topology) QdiscShow(name string) string {
330		p := top.path(name)
331		return top.runOut("tc", "qdisc", "show", "dev", p.edgeVeth)
332	}
333	
334	// LinkExists reports whether the edge-side veth of the named path still exists.
335	func (top *Topology) LinkExists(name string) bool {
336		p := top.path(name)
337		return top.tryRun("ip", "link", "show", p.edgeVeth) == nil
338	}
339	
340	// Teardown removes the topology. Killing the holder drops the concentrator netns
341	// (and its veth ends); the edge-side veths are deleted explicitly. Idempotent.
342	func (top *Topology) Teardown() {
343		for _, p := range top.paths {
344			_ = top.tryRun("ip", "link", "del", p.edgeVeth)
345		}
346		if top.holder != nil && top.holder.Process != nil {
347			_ = top.holder.Process.Kill()
348			_, _ = top.holder.Process.Wait()
349			top.holder = nil
350		}
351	}
352	
353	func (top *Topology) run(name string, args ...string) {
354		top.t.Helper()
355		if out, err := exec.Command(name, args...).CombinedOutput(); err != nil {
356			top.t.Fatalf("%s %s: %v\n%s", name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
357		}
358	}
359	
360	func (top *Topology) tryRun(name string, args ...string) error {
361		return exec.Command(name, args...).Run()
362	}
363	
364	func (top *Topology) runOut(name string, args ...string) string {
365		top.t.Helper()
366		out, err := exec.Command(name, args...).CombinedOutput()
367		if err != nil {
368			top.t.Fatalf("%s %s: %v\n%s", name, strings.Join(args, " "), err, out)
369		}
370		return string(out)
371	}
372	
373	// iperfListenTimeout bounds waitIperfListen: how long to wait for the one-shot
374	// iperf3 server to reach the LISTEN state before failing the test.
375	const iperfListenTimeout = 5 * time.Second
376	
377	// waitIperfListen polls, inside the concentrator network namespace, for a TCP
378	// socket in the LISTEN state on the given port, and returns once it appears (or
379	// fails the test at iperfListenTimeout). It reads kernel socket state via
380	// `ss -ltn` and NEVER connects: the iperf3 servers here run one-shot (`-s -1`),
381	// so a probe-connect would consume the server's single accept and break the real
382	// client. It replaces the prior fixed sleeps at the iperf3Mbps and rttUnderLoad
383	// call sites, which raced a slow bind under load into 'connection refused' (D3).
384	func (top *Topology) waitIperfListen(t *testing.T, port int) {
385		t.Helper()
386		suffix := fmt.Sprintf(":%d", port)
387		deadline := time.Now().Add(iperfListenTimeout)
388		for time.Now().Before(deadline) {
389			// ss -ltn columns: State Recv-Q Send-Q Local-Address:Port Peer-Address:Port.
390			out, err := exec.Command("nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", "ss", "-ltn").CombinedOutput()
391			if err == nil {
392				for _, line := range strings.Split(string(out), "\n") {
393					fields := strings.Fields(line)
394					if len(fields) >= 4 && strings.HasSuffix(fields[3], suffix) {
395						return
396					}
397				}
398			}
399			time.Sleep(20 * time.Millisecond)
400		}
401		t.Fatalf("iperf3 server never reached LISTEN on port %d within %s", port, iperfListenTimeout)
402	}
403	
404	// nsenter runs a command inside the concentrator network namespace.
405	func (top *Topology) nsenter(args ...string) {
406		top.t.Helper()
407		full := append([]string{"-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n"}, args...)
408		top.run("nsenter", full...)
409	}

22	// T60 is the netns e2e for the W1 startup-resilience feature END TO END: T51's
23	// tolerant Open() (defer a well-formed but not-yet-assignable source_addr instead of
24	// refusing the whole bond) composed with T55's background reconcile (promote a
25	// deferred path to live once its address appears), plus the two fast-fail edges
26	// (zero bindable paths; a malformed source_addr) that tolerance must NOT paper over.
27	
28	const (
29		// t60MetricsListen is the edge's /metrics endpoint for this file's tests, on a
30		// port none of the other e2e files use (see test/e2e/p2/p3/p4/pacing for 9095-97).
31		t60MetricsListen = "127.0.0.1:9098"
32		t60MetricsURL    = "http://" + t60MetricsListen + "/metrics"
33	
34		// t60UnassignableSrcA/B mirror internal/bind/tolerant_bind_test.go's
35		// unassignableSource: 192.0.2.0/24 is TEST-NET-1 (RFC 5737), reserved for
36		// documentation and guaranteed never configured on a real interface, so a bind
37		// against it deterministically fails EADDRNOTAVAIL without any netns fixture.
38		t60UnassignableSrcA = "192.0.2.1"
39		t60UnassignableSrcB = "192.0.2.2"
40	)
41	
42	// t60DeferredPath is the THIRD emulated uplink (distinct veth names from
43	// DefaultPaths' starlink/cellular): its edge address is deliberately withheld at
44	// Setup (deferEdgeAddr), so wbCe exists and is up but 10.100.3.1 is not yet owned by
45	// any interface — the well-formed-but-not-yet-assignable condition T51 defers.
46	// top.AddEdgeAddr("modem5g") later adds it, simulating the address appearing.
47	var t60DeferredPath = pathSpec{
48		name: "modem5g", edgeIP: "10.100.3.1", concIP: "10.100.3.2",
49		edgeVeth: "wbCe", concVeth: "wbCc", delayMs: 20,
50		deferEdgeAddr: true,
51	}
52	
53	// t60PromoteTimeout bounds how long the T55 background reconcile may take to bind,
54	// promote, and bring the deferred path's liveness to Up after its address appears:
55	// one worst-case reconcile tick (bind.DefaultReconcileInterval, the poll cadence)
56	// plus the liveness up-transition (DefaultUpSuccesses consecutive probe echoes at
57	// DefaultProbeInterval), plus harness slack for scheduling jitter under the fixture's
58	// shared CPU — mirroring the analytical-bound style of thresholds.go's
59	// PLivenessFailoverBudget/PLivenessDetectBudget.
60	const t60PromoteTimeout = bind.DefaultReconcileInterval +
61		time.Duration(telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses)*telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval +
62		3*time.Second
63	
64	// TestTolerantStartupDeferredPathPromotes is the T60 acceptance: bring the bond up
65	// with TWO paths where one (modem5g) names a well-formed source_addr that is not yet
66	// assignable on any interface. The bond must come up on the survivor (starlink) —
67	// handshake + traffic — while modem5g is present in config but excluded from the
68	// live path set (not fatal). Adding the missing address must then let T55's
69	// background reconcile bind, promote, and carry traffic on modem5g.
70	func TestTolerantStartupDeferredPathPromotes(t *testing.T) {
71		bin := buildWanbond(t)
72		survivor := DefaultPaths[0] // starlink
73		paths := []pathSpec{survivor, t60DeferredPath}
74		top := SetupWithPaths(t, paths)
75		edge, conc := setupT60Tunnel(t, top, bin, paths, t60MetricsListen)
76	
77		// (1) The bond comes up on the survivor alone: modem5g's source_addr is not yet
78		// assignable, so its bind deferred rather than failing the whole Open (T51).
79		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, 15*time.Second) {
80			t.Fatalf("bond never came up on the survivor path %q while %q was deferred\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
81				survivor.name, t60DeferredPath.name, edge.log(), conc.log())
82		}
83		mbps := top.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, 3)
84		if mbps <= 0 {
85			t.Fatalf("survivor path %q carried non-positive throughput %.2f Mbit/s", survivor.name, mbps)
86		}
87	
88		// The deferred path is PRESENT in config but excluded from the live/served set:
89		// bind.Multipath.PathSnapshots() — what the /metrics endpoint scrapes — reports
90		// only BOUND paths (m.paths), so a still-deferred path's wanbond_path_up series is
91		// simply ABSENT, not zero. This operationalizes "present-but-down, not fatal":
92		// the daemon is running, the survivor is up, and the deferred path has no live
93		// series until T55 promotes it.
94		before := scrapeMetrics(t, t60MetricsURL)
95		if _, ok := before.PathValue(metrics.MetricUp, t60DeferredPath.name); ok {
96			t.Fatalf("deferred path %q already has a wanbond_path_up series before its address was added; it should be absent (not yet promoted)", t60DeferredPath.name)
97		}
98		if v, ok := before.PathValue(metrics.MetricUp, survivor.name); !ok || v != 1 {
99			t.Fatalf("survivor path %q wanbond_path_up = %v (present=%v), want 1", survivor.name, v, ok)
100		}
101		t.Logf("pre-promote: survivor %q up (iperf3=%.1f Mbit/s), deferred %q absent from live metrics as expected", survivor.name, mbps, t60DeferredPath.name)
102	
103		// (2) The missing address appears — e.g. the 5G modem's DHCP lease completes.
104		top.AddEdgeAddr(t60DeferredPath.name)
105	
106		// T55's background reconcile must bind, promote, and bring the path's liveness to
107		// Up within the analytical budget above.
108		waitPathUp(t, t60MetricsURL, t60DeferredPath.name, 1, t60PromoteTimeout)
109	
110		// Prove the PROMOTED path itself carries traffic (not merely that the bond
111		// survives): blackhole the survivor so only the reconciled path can forward. The
112		// active-backup scheduler's Pick can only land here once modem5g is genuinely
113		// admitted and healthy.
114		top.Blackhole(survivor.name)
115		if !top.pingUntil(concInner, time.Duration(P1RecoverySeconds)*time.Second+2*time.Second) {
116			t.Fatalf("tunnel did not recover on the promoted path %q once the survivor %q was blackholed\n--- edge ---\n%s\n--- conc ---\n%s",
117				t60DeferredPath.name, survivor.name, edge.log(), conc.log())
118		}
119		promotedMbps := top.iperf3Mbps(t, concInner, 3)
120		if promotedMbps <= 0 {
121			t.Fatalf("promoted path %q carried non-positive throughput %.2f Mbit/s", t60DeferredPath.name, promotedMbps)
122		}
123		top.Restore(survivor.name)
124	
125		t.Logf("T60: survivor %q up first (iperf3=%.1f Mbit/s) while %q was deferred; after its address was added, T55 promoted it and it alone carried iperf3=%.1f Mbit/s",
126			survivor.name, mbps, t60DeferredPath.name, promotedMbps)
127	}
128	
129	// TestTolerantStartupFastFailModes asserts the two edges tolerance must NOT paper
130	// over: zero bindable paths (every source_addr unassignable) is a FATAL Open, and a
131	// malformed source_addr is rejected at config LOAD, before the daemon ever attempts a
132	// bind. Both run in the test-process network namespace (the TestMain re-exec unshared
133	// it); neither needs the veth topology, since both source addresses are TEST-NET-1
134	// (RFC 5737, never assignable on any real interface) or simply invalid syntax.
135	func TestTolerantStartupFastFailModes(t *testing.T) {
136		bin := buildWanbond(t)
137	
138		t.Run("zero_bindable_paths_is_fatal", func(t *testing.T) {
139			// Determinism (hardware-robustness fix): the zero-bindable premise is that BOTH
140			// source_addrs fail to bind, i.e. a bind to a non-local address returns
141			// EADDRNOTAVAIL. On this kernel that rejection needs TWO conditions, BOTH pinned
142			// here so the premise holds regardless of host default or subtest order:
143			//   (1) at least one interface UP — with lo DOWN a non-local bind SUCCEEDS even at
144			//       ip_nonlocal_bind=0 (empirical kernel probe on llm-ubuntu-0); this subtest
145			//       builds no topology, so it must bring lo up itself (bringLoopbackUp);
146			//   (2) net.ipv4.ip_nonlocal_bind=0 — a host at 1 lets a non-local bind succeed
147			//       (disableNonlocalBind, which fails loudly if it cannot pin the value).
148			// Together they make "no configured path can bind -> fatal Open" deterministic.
149			// The original fix pinned only (2) and mis-identified the variable, so the daemon
150			// came up (both TEST-NET-1 addresses bound) and the test hung — the false-negative
151			// observed on llm-ubuntu-0 when this subtest ran alone (lo down).
152			bringLoopbackUp(t)
153			disableNonlocalBind(t)
154	
155			cfg := writeT60EdgeConfig(t, fmt.Sprintf(`[[paths]]
156	name = "a"
157	source_addr = "%s"
158	
159	[[paths]]
160	name = "b"
161	source_addr = "%s"
162	`, t60UnassignableSrcA, t60UnassignableSrcB))
163	
164			code, out := runWanbondOnce(t, bin, cfg, 10*time.Second)
165			if code == 0 {
166				t.Fatalf("wanbond exited 0 with zero bindable paths (both %s/%s are TEST-NET-1, never assignable); want a fatal non-zero exit\n%s",
167					t60UnassignableSrcA, t60UnassignableSrcB, out)
168			}
169			if !strings.Contains(out, "wanbond starting") {
170				t.Fatalf("expected the daemon to log startup before failing at Open — that is what distinguishes this fatal-Open case from a config-load error; output:\n%s", out)
171			}
172			if !strings.Contains(out, "no configured path could bind") {
173				t.Fatalf("expected the bind's zero-bindable fatal error message; output:\n%s", out)
174			}
175			t.Logf("zero-bindable-paths: wanbond exited %d as expected:\n%s", code, out)
176		})
177	
178		t.Run("malformed_source_addr_is_config_load_error", func(t *testing.T) {
179			cfg := writeT60EdgeConfig(t, `[[paths]]
180	name = "a"
181	source_addr = "not-an-ip-address"
182	`)
183	
184			code, out := runWanbondOnce(t, bin, cfg, 10*time.Second)
185			if code == 0 {
186				t.Fatalf("wanbond exited 0 with a malformed source_addr; want a config-load error\n%s", out)
187			}
188			if strings.Contains(out, "wanbond starting") {
189				t.Fatalf("a malformed source_addr must be rejected at config LOAD, before the daemon starts (the startup log should never appear); output:\n%s", out)
190			}
191			if !strings.Contains(out, "invalid source_addr") {
192				t.Fatalf("expected config.normalize's invalid-source_addr error; output:\n%s", out)
193			}
194			t.Logf("malformed-source_addr: wanbond exited %d at config load as expected:\n%s", code, out)
195		})
196	}
197	
198	// writeT60EdgeConfig writes a minimal, otherwise-valid edge config (role, psk,
199	// wireguard private/peer key, a bogus-but-syntactically-fine peer endpoint) with
200	// pathsBlock spliced in as the [[paths]] section(s) — the ONLY thing under test in
201	// TestTolerantStartupFastFailModes. Neither subtest reaches the peer, so the
202	// endpoint/allowed_ips values need not be routable.
203	//
204	// [log] level is "info" (NOT "error"): the zero-bindable subtest distinguishes a
205	// fatal Open() from a config-load failure by asserting the "wanbond starting" marker
206	// (main.Info, cmd/wanbond/main.go) IS present — that marker is logged at Info, so an
207	// "error" level would suppress it and the assertion could never hold (the daemon runs
208	// but logs nothing at Info). The malformed-source_addr subtest still sees NO
209	// "wanbond starting" because config load fails BEFORE that log, independent of level.
210	func writeT60EdgeConfig(t *testing.T, pathsBlock string) string {
211		t.Helper()
212		edgePriv, _ := genKey(t)
213		_, concPub := genKey(t)
214		psk := randKey(t)
215		dir := t.TempDir()
216		return writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edge.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
217	psk = "%s"
218	
219	%s
220	[wireguard]
221	private_key = "%s"
222	
223	[[wireguard.peers]]
224	public_key = "%s"
225	endpoint = "192.0.2.53:51820"
226	allowed_ips = ["10.10.0.2/32"]
227	
228	[log]
229	level = "info"
230	`, psk, pathsBlock, edgePriv, concPub))
231	}
232	
233	// runWanbondOnce runs bin --config cfgPath to completion — no signal is sent; the
234	// process must exit ON ITS OWN — within deadline, and returns its exit code and
235	// combined stdout+stderr. It fails the test if the process does not exit within the
236	// deadline: for these two fast-fail scenarios a hang would mean Open (or config
237	// load) blocked instead of failing fast, which is itself a defect worth surfacing
238	// loudly rather than silently killing the process and reporting a false pass.
239	func runWanbondOnce(t *testing.T, bin, cfgPath string, deadline time.Duration) (exitCode int, output string) {
240		t.Helper()
241		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), deadline)
242		defer cancel()
243		cmd := exec.CommandContext(ctx, bin, "--config", cfgPath)
244		out, err := cmd.CombinedOutput()
245		if ctx.Err() == context.DeadlineExceeded {
246			t.Fatalf("wanbond did not exit within %s (expected a fast fail); output so far:\n%s", deadline, out)
247		}
248		if err == nil {
249			return 0, string(out)
250		}
251		var ee *exec.ExitError
252		if errors.As(err, &ee) {
253			return ee.ExitCode(), string(out)
254		}
255		t.Fatalf("run wanbond --config %s: %v\n%s", cfgPath, err, out)
256		return -1, string(out)
257	}
258	
259	// setupT60Tunnel is setupMultipathTunnelLevel plus an edge [metrics] listen block:
260	// T60 needs to scrape the edge's per-path wanbond_path_up series to observe the
261	// deferred path being absent, then promoted. It otherwise mirrors
262	// setupMultipathTunnelLevel's config shape and bring-up sequence exactly.
263	func setupT60Tunnel(t *testing.T, top *Topology, bin string, paths []pathSpec, metricsListen string) (edge, conc *proc) {
264		t.Helper()
265	
266		edgePriv, edgePub := genKey(t)
267		concPriv, concPub := genKey(t)
268		psk := randKey(t)
269	
270		var edgePaths, concPaths strings.Builder
271		for _, p := range paths {
272			fmt.Fprintf(&edgePaths, "[[paths]]\nname = %q\nsource_addr = %q\ndest_addr = \"%s:%d\"\n\n", p.name, p.edgeIP, p.concIP, listenPort)
273			fmt.Fprintf(&concPaths, "[[paths]]\nname = %q\nsource_addr = %q\n\n", p.name, p.concIP)
274		}
275		// The wireguard peer endpoint (edge -> concentrator) seeds the virtual endpoint;
276		// the first configured path's concentrator address serves (mirrors
277		// setupMultipathTunnelLevel).
278		primary := paths[0]
279		metricsBlock := fmt.Sprintf("[metrics]\nlisten = %q\n\n", metricsListen)
280	
281		dir := t.TempDir()
282		edgeCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "edge.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "edge"
283	psk = "%s"
284	
285	%s%s[wireguard]
286	private_key = "%s"
287	
288	[[wireguard.peers]]
289	public_key = "%s"
290	endpoint = "%s:%d"
291	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
292	
293	[log]
294	level = "error"
295	`, psk, edgePaths.String(), metricsBlock, edgePriv, concPub, primary.concIP, listenPort, concInner))
296	
297		concCfg := writeConfig(t, filepath.Join(dir, "conc.toml"), fmt.Sprintf(`role = "concentrator"
298	psk = "%s"
299	
300	%s[wireguard]
301	private_key = "%s"
302	listen_port = %d
303	
304	[[wireguard.peers]]
305	public_key = "%s"
306	allowed_ips = ["%s/32"]
307	
308	[log]
309	level = "error"
310	`, psk, concPaths.String(), concPriv, listenPort, edgePub, edgeInner))
311	
312		conc = top.startProc(t, "concentrator", "nsenter", "-t", strconv.Itoa(top.pid), "-n", bin, "--config", concCfg)
313		edge = top.startProc(t, "edge", bin, "--config", edgeCfg)
314	
315		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, false, 5*time.Second) {
316			t.Fatalf("edge %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, edge.log())
317		}
318		if !top.waitLink(tunDev, true, 5*time.Second) {
319			t.Fatalf("concentrator %s never appeared\n%s", tunDev, conc.log())
320		}
321		top.run("ip", "addr", "add", edgeInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
322		top.run("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
323		top.nsenter("ip", "addr", "add", concInner+"/24", "dev", tunDev)
324		top.nsenter("ip", "link", "set", tunDev, "up")
325		return edge, conc
326	}
327	
328	// scrapeMetrics is a one-shot scrape of url, failing the test on any error (used
329	// once the endpoint is already known to be up — unlike waitPathUp's tolerant poll).
330	func scrapeMetrics(t *testing.T, url string) metrics.Exposition {
331		t.Helper()
332		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
333		defer cancel()
334		exp, err := metrics.Fetch(ctx, http.DefaultClient, url)
335		if err != nil {
336			t.Fatalf("scrape %s: %v", url, err)
337		}
338		return exp
339	}
340	
341	// waitPathUp polls url's /metrics until the named path's wanbond_path_up series
342	// reads want, or fails the test at deadline. A scrape error is tolerated mid-poll
343	// (transient — never the terminal condition), since the assertion is about the
344	// SERIES VALUE appearing, not about the endpoint's own availability (already proven
345	// up by an earlier successful scrape in the caller).
346	func waitPathUp(t *testing.T, url, path string, want float64, deadline time.Duration) {
347		t.Helper()
348		end := time.Now().Add(deadline)
349		var lastErr error
350		for time.Now().Before(end) {
351			ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 1*time.Second)
352			exp, err := metrics.Fetch(ctx, http.DefaultClient, url)
353			cancel()
354			if err != nil {
355				lastErr = err
356			} else if v, ok := exp.PathValue(metrics.MetricUp, path); ok && v == want {
357				return
358			}
359			time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
360		}
361		t.Fatalf("wanbond_path_up{path=%q} never reached %v within %s (last scrape error: %v)", path, want, deadline, lastErr)
362	}
